title: A. Density and Mix of Uses
story: Transit-oriented development centers dense, mixed-use places within walking distance of transit, scaled to their surrounding context. By clustering homes, jobs, shops, schools, and entertainment near stations, TOD boosts ridership, improves transit efficiency, and reduces car reliance. This mix can happen in one building, across blocks, or around nearby stations.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighbourhood with mixed use buildings highlighted

title: B. Equitable Development
story: Equitable transit-oriented development pairs transit access with affordable mixed-income housing, better job access, and community-focused investment. It helps reduce displacement, lower housing and transportation costs, and expand access to opportunity by reducing car dependence and long commutes. Equitable TOD also supports transit-centered job hubs and last-mile connections, while strengthening local businesses, services, and climate-resilient development.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighbourhood with affordable housing and public transportation highlighted

title: C. A Great Public Realm
story: A great public realm supports TOD by making station areas safe, accessible, and inviting for walking, biking, and transit connections. Features like complete streets, sidewalks, crossings, plazas, lighting, wayfinding, and active ground-floor uses improve access for pedestrians, cyclists, seniors, and people with disabilities while creating lively, connected places. These improvements are often funded through developer contributions, grants, and public financing tools.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighbourhood with bike lane, crosswalk, and wheelchair accessible ramps highlighted

title: D. A TOD Approach to Parking
story: A TOD parking approach provides only the parking actually needed and manages it so it does not dominate station areas. By supporting transit, walking, and mixed-use development, TOD can reduce parking demand, lower costs, and free land for housing, jobs, shops, and public space. Instead of eliminating parking, it uses lower ratios, shared facilities, strategic park-and-ride, and demand-management programs.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighbourhood with parking lot highlighted
